channel.title: math on mahmood
channel.description: Recent content in math on mahmood

item.title: convolutional neural network
item.description: this is still very much a work in progress some of the math notation may be inconsistent, but things are valid and make sense when read in context (i should fix that though) citations (Peter Norvig, Stuart J. Russell, 2020) (Jianxin Wu, 2017) (Zhifei Zhang, 2016) and countless other resources that helped me form a good understanding of the topics related to conv networks motivation an image cannot be thought of as a simple vector of input pixel values, primarily because adjacency of pixels in an image matters.

item.title: logic programming in common lisp
item.description: this document needs some polishing!, the backward chaining algorithm isnt finished for example logic programming in common lisp logical statements we make use of the symbolic capabilities of lisp, we use quoting and symbols to represent logical statements, e.g. given the following knowledge base of horn clauses: in lisp we could write it as: (defparameter *kb* '((implies p q) (implies (and l m) p) (implies (and b l) m) (implies (and a p) l) (implies (and a b s) l) A B)) *kb* ((IMPLIES P Q) (IMPLIES (AND L M) P) (IMPLIES (AND B L) M) (IMPLIES (AND A P) L) (IMPLIES (AND A B S) L) A B) the choice of the symbols implies, and could vary, but essentially we should write code that deals properly with these symbols based on their names.

item.title: artificial intelligence homework 4
item.description: all the code used here can be found at https://mahmoodsheikh.github.io assume you have the following knowledge create a decision tree based on the knowledge base above (ignore direction). the graph with the electric layout algorithm: (defparameter *homework4-knowledge-base* '((implies (and a b ) e ) (implies (and a c ) k ) (implies (and a c d) f ) (implies (and c d ) g ) (implies (and e f ) h ) (implies (and f g ) i ) (implies (and f e g) j ) (implies (and k l ) (and m n)) (implies (and f d ) l ) a b c)) (graph-generate-tikz (generate-graph-from-knowledge-base *homework4-knowledge-base*) :directed t :node-distance 60) the same graph with the layered layout algorithm: (graph-generate-tikz (generate-graph-from-knowledge-base *homework4-knowledge-base*) :directed t :tikz-params "layered layout, grow=up, edges={-{Stealth[]}}, edges=rounded corners, level sep=40px") using backward chaining, try to prove using , and as the data.

item.title: unification
item.description: the algorithm (shown in 1) takes two sentences and returns a unifier for them (a substitution) if one exists: let us look at some examples of how should behave. suppose we have a query : whom does John know? answers to this query can be found by finding all sentences in the knowledge base that unify with . here are the results of unification with four different sentences that might be in the knowledge base: the last unification fails because cannot take on the values John and Elizabeth at the same time.

item.title: forward chaining
item.description: a family of inference algorithms propositional logic chaining the forward-chaining algorithm (which stands for “propositional logic forward chaining entails” and is shown in 1) determines if a single proposition symbol –the query–is entailed by a knowledge base of definite clauses. it begins from known facts (positive literals) in the knowledge base. if all the premises of an implication are known, then its conclusion is added to the set of known facts.

item.title: 2d transformations
item.description: we usually use a vector to represent a point in 2d space a general form of linear transformations (or more precisely, affine transformations) in can be wrriten as: or or, in matrix form, using homogeneous coordinates: matrices allow arbitrary linear transformations to be displayed in a consistent format, suitable for computation 2d translation given a point , we wanna reposition it with the translation distance this is an affine transformation, we deduce an affine transformation matrix: 2d rotation about the origin a rotation about the origin by angle can be done with the matrix: 2d scaling scaling is just multiplying the variables by some constants, it is defined using the cross product of the point by the scaling vector we deduce the transformation matrix: using homogeneous coordinates: linearly mapping a grid to another simplifying the formula from linearly mapping an interval onto another to 2 dimensions: mapping the point from the intervals , respectively, to the intervals , respectively

item.title: linearly mapping an interval onto another
item.description: basically the idea is that, given an real interval and the number such that and another interval , we wanna find the number such that we need to isolate : extending to higher dimensions assume a vector space with a dimension of , given the 2 sets of real intervals and a vector : where for all is bound in the interval , i.e. we wanna find the vector : such that so we write the linear transformation (might not be necessarily linear but affine) obviously this function doesnt preserve the origin as , so its an affine transformation, we separate the intercept so we can drop it and add it later: we drop the intercept and find the transformation matrix of the function without it, which is a square matrix of size im taking it for granted that the without the intercept is linear, in reality we need to check if the properties of a linear function are preserved in then we add the intercept with homogeneous coordinates to get the final matrix, which is a square matrix of size so to conclude, the formula transforms the vector from a given set of intervals onto another, resulting in

item.title: artificial intelligence homework 2
item.description: given the following game tree original tree image from homework: implement pruning, show the optimal tree and the pruned subtrees (explain) a step by step execution, at each step the modified node and the nodes that it depends on to determine its value are highlighted red, the assumption here is that the depth-first search algorithm prefers the left side (applies recursion to left node first, then right node), next to each node written are the values of for the state associated with it, the pruned nodes are colored blue.

item.title: artificial intelligence homework 3
item.description: scroll to the end if you’re only interested in the solution and the steps in raw text.. homework on constraint satisfaction problem using common lisp graphs graph image from homework: gotta solve this as an rgba map coloring problem make use of forward checking prioritize vertices with least constraining (least edges, least constraints) to most constraining (most edges), in case of a draw, use the variable with the most remaining colors (least constrained), if there is another tie, choose the vertex to the left first we define the graph: (defparameter *mygraph* (make-graph :adjacency-list '((a .

item.title: decision tree
item.description: <&aima2010> a decision tree represents a function that takes as input a vector of attribute values and returns a “decision”–a single output value. the input and output values can be discrete or continuous for a wide variety of problems, the decision tree yields a nice, concise result. but some functions cannot be represented concisely, for example, the majority function, which returns true if and only if more than half of the inputs are true, requires an exponentially large decision tree.

item.title: constraint satisfaction problem
item.description: refer to [chapter 6] a constraint satisfaction problem consists of three components, , , and : is a set of variables, is a set of domains, , one for each variable is a set of constraints that specify allowable combinations of values a domain, , consists of a set of allowable values, , for variable . for example, a boolean variable would have the domain . different variables can have different domains of different sizes.

item.title: learning math functions with neural networks
item.description: see polynomial regression quick demo linear regression i could’ve used my code for multivariate linear regression but i wanna study how neural networks can do regression tasks if we were to try approximating a linear function using a simple multilayer perceptron we would see that since most activation functions squash the values of the network into a small range like in case of the sigmoid, it would be hard to regress to functions whose codomain is different that that of the activation functions, so we probably want to use a special activation function, or even easier, drop activation functions altogether, this way the neural network would basically be doing linear regression lets begin by trying to learn a linear function, for that we need to be able to generate random linear functions, perhaps with some noise, so we write a simple function for that: (defun generate-points-along-function (fn noise-variance n &optional (range 1000)) "generate n points along a function with some random noise.

item.title: maxima in lisp
item.description: i ended up giving up on using maxima as a visualization/symbolic math computation library for lisp, it wasnt made with this usecase in mind i guess, and so its an utter nightmare to lisp with it maxima is written in lisp and so it can be used as a modified/extended lisp runtime how to to start the lisp runtime use the command: rmaxima -r 'to_lisp();' can also run this in emacs’ slime/sly with (sly "rmaxima -r to_lisp();") alternatively, we can clone maxima’s source code to the subdirectory local-projects of the quicklisp installation directory (usually ~/quicklisp unless you’ve modified it), follow the instructions at https://sourceforge.
